data.title: WK Kellogg: Minimal Risks And Strong Upside Ahead
data.description:
WK Kellogg Co (NYSE:KLG) is a mid-cap stock that has caught my attention due to its attractive valuation and positioning in the consumer staples sector. As I have written about before on Seeking Alpha, I am anticipating a value transition from growth to value as indices become increasingly overbought by exuberant bulls. This is not to say that I believe that a correction is headed any time soon (as nobody can predict this), but rather that I think liquidity is in the process of moving towards small and mid-cap companies as they offer better value for money per dollar invested. KLG to me is among the relatively small universe of stocks that I believe still offer a good deal to investors, and it presents a compelling opportunity as a newly independent company undergoing a significant transformation. With a forward P/E of 11.58x, a 3.63% dividend yield, and a clear path to margin expansion through its $500M supply chain modernization, KLG ticks many of my boxes and more, and I am pleased to enthusiastically provide KLG with a Buy recommendation.

Company Overview

KLG is perhaps among the most perennial and most visible consumer staples stocks that North Americans interact with in their everyday lives. Crucially, it spun off from Kellanova in October last year and now is an independent identity with a streamlined management team to unlock value from its operations. KLG is a staple of the North American breakfast, with its household names of Frosted Flakes, Special K, Froot Loops, and others featuring prominently in cupboards and grocers across the country. Despite its parent companies' 118-year history, its product line has remained robust, as the company boasts a 27.6% market share in the U.S. cereal category, while in Canada, its market share is significantly higher at 39%.

KLG has six primary manufacturing facilities throughout the U.S., which are then distributed to its customers, which include merchandisers, grocers, drug stores, and many others across the country. This delivery process is logistically intensive, and under Kellanova's wing, it has become a sprawling, (needlessly) complicated and outdated venture. To summarize a few key issues as outlined in its recent earnings call.

  • The Omaha facility has an aging infrastructure due to it being one of the oldest plants in its network. This is increasing KLG's operating costs via inefficient production processes.
  • Its Memphis facility is also reportedly facing challenges due to its excessive rice production.
  • On a broader level, its manufacturing hubs are scattered throughout the U.S. The logistical costs are high due to their geographic spread.

The good news for investors is that there are simple solutions to these issues, and simplicity is the solution that management has decided to go with. Its Omaha plant is due to be closed by 2026, while production at its Memphis plant will be scaled back by 2025. These optimizations are part of KLG's broader, $500 million supply chain modernization program aimed at expanding EBITDA margins from 9% to approximately 14% post 2026. Around $390 million of that $500 million will be spent on modernizing and installing new equipment at its facilities, with the remainder earmarked to streamline its logistics and production on a higher level. The intended net result is to have a smaller, simpler, and more reliable supply chain that costs less to operate and is easier to maintain.

What I like about this opportunity is the absence of excessive speculation on behalf of management as well as the minimization of undefined risk. The company's plan is concrete; a reader can follow along with the plan and logically deduce how cost savings could be reached.

To me, management revealing this plan shows that the company is already unlocking the expected value from its spin-off and becoming an independent entity, allowing management to respond faster due to the reduced overhead. There are some promising signs too in KLG's recent financials that suggest that an EBITDA expansion of 14% by 2026 is a realistic opportunity.

Financials

KLG has maintained their guidance for the rest of the year, but it will now be near the lower end. There is some cyclicality to its business, namely that typically during Q4 it's their lowest-volume quarter. However, on an adjusted basis, EBITDA is expected to grow between 3-5%.

Much of KLG's expected EBITDA growth I think can be chalked up to its strong gross margin expansion. The firm reported a 30% gross margin in Q2 of this year, driven by quick wins in operational improvements.

Something that, I think, is important here is that KLG is actually one of the best performances in recent years in terms of gross margin, so I believe that the firm is in a better position than what it appears. This is per comments taken from CFO Dave McKinstray:

Our underlying improvement is the direct result of our end-to-end focus and sustained improvements in our supply chain operations, including ongoing waste reductions. Gross margin for the second quarter was 30%, a 100-basis-point decline versus the prior year. Excluding the insurance proceeds, gross margin expanded over 100 basis points. Our underlying improvement is the result of our end-to-end focus and sustained improvements in our supply chain operations, including ongoing waste reductions.

However, there are some issues with KLG's financials, notably, that the firm reported significant volume declines last quarter at a 4.8% drop. It was highlighted by management that this drop was both unusual and somewhat alarming due to this drop occurring while its market share remained constant, which suggests that it's not a company-specific issue but due to a consumer pushback on price hikes for cereal products broadly. For the company, pain can be felt the most in its Special K brand, with its share reducing by 40 basis points year-to-date.

Other concerns for me is that KLG has guided for negative free cash flow this year, which will see its financial flexibility constrained over the next few years. It's still dealing with the burden of becoming an independent company (which cost around $60 million) and is working through its modernization spending of around $45 million. Thus, through its combination of it going through a transformative stage and net debt of $447 million, it expects its leverage to peak at 3x adjusted EBITDA in early 2026. Accounts receivable have grown to $313 million compared to $266 million in December, and its supplier finance program has also swelled to $158 million from $142 million in the same period. Due to these factors, I do not foresee any dividend growth for KLG in the near future, but what I do see is that its stock price will continue along its solid trajectory to head higher.

Valuation

KLG has had an impressive year-to-date, with its stock price climbing 31.28% at the time of writing. Much of that enthusiasm can, of course, be chalked up to it being a newly spun-off company and it reaping its early optimization benefits.

On a longer timescale, KLG's stock price has increased even more at 64.60% over the past year and 17.51% over the past five years. Despite this valuation increase, it still remains competitively priced compared to peers in its sector.

The company holds an A- rating on Seeking Alpha's quant rating for its valuation, with it being perceptibly undervalued based on EV/EBITDA and earnings metrics.

The consensus among analysts is also very positive, with its EPS expected to swell to 24% by December 2026 and to 43.29% to December 2027. If one were to take these two metrics into consideration, one could say that KLG is undervalued now and for the foreseeable future.

KLG might be trading at a discount due to its concerns as well as capital flowing into more attractive growth-based investments as the bull market rages on. However, I believe that KLG could be undervalued from a qualitative perspective as well. It fits into the sweet spot for investors looking for a pure-play staples stock that is also on a solid track to significantly improve its margins and profitability, with a reasonable and concrete plan to reach it. Installing new equipment and improving its logistics network are straightforward steps compared with the more ambitious (and far riskier) plans than many of its peers, and these steps also lead to tangible cost savings and greater efficiencies as well. It's a buy for investors looking for a company that is easy to understand and is going places.

Risks

Although I rate the overall execution risks of KLG's supply chain modernization plan on the low side, what I do see as a potential roadblock is capital constraints and lack of financial flexibility. As I noted earlier, it has a significant amount of net debt, and it is already tapping into its accounts receivables and credit accounts to navigate its transformation. According to the company's reports and earnings call, it has $644 in accessible capital, which includes $44 million in cash, a delayed term loan of $250 million, and a revolving credit facility of $350 million. When one combines its total transformation costs of $500 million, stand-up costs, and dividend payments over three years, it more than exceeds its total cash available, meaning that it will need to use these funds as well as its operating cash flow to fund its commitments.

Even a perfectly managed plan can go awry, and KLG's plans are significant. I believe then that there's a significant risk that either that the company will need to raise additional funds, or (as a more likely case) that the transformation will happen over a longer time frame than what has been stipulated. All available dry powder that the company has to work with has already been accounted for due to its plans. Investors must then keep the bigger picture in mind and accept the possibility that it may not unfold as effortlessly as management is predicting due to its liquidity position.

Takeaway

I like KLG due to the strength of its stock price, expanding margins, and its common-sense approach to unlocking thicker margins for investors. I anticipate that it may be a bumpy ride in seeing its supply chain modernization program through to its conclusion, but the overall execution risks seem minimal due to the plan's simplicity - although this does not imply it will be easy. The company's valuation is attractive, and if investors hold on tight, I believe that their KLG investment could be worth significantly more in the coming years.

data.title: YMAG: An Unconventional Method To Amplifying Your Portfolio Income
data.description:
Overview

I've covered some of the YieldMax funds in the past, and I am generally onboard with the inclusion of these high-yielding option ETFs. The problem with these 'ETFs' is that they still have an elevated risk profile since they are essentially linked to a single stock, rather than an index. YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund Of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) helps to alleviate some of these concerns by aiming to provide an underlying exposure to the Magnificent 7. For reference, the Mag 7 includes the following stocks:

  1. Apple (AAPL)
  2. Microsoft (MSFT)
  3. Amazon (AMZN)
  4. Alphabet (GOOG)
  5. Nvidia (NVDA)
  6. Tesla (TSLA)
  7. Meta Platforms (META)

YieldMax has created an option fund for each of these 7 stocks and the results have varied based on their respective levels of volatility, NAV health, and performance of the underlying asset. While there are some outliers in this group of holdings, we can see that YMAG has held up quite nice in terms of a total return comparison. On a YTD basis, YMAG has delivered a 22.5% total return as a result of the price downside and high rate of distributions to offset. Part of the appeal of YMAG is that it sports a massive dividend yield greater than 62%, according to the latest fund overview.

As a disclosure, I do plan to initiate a position in YMAG within a thirty-day span. Despite this, I aim to share an unbiased perspective on the benefits of this sort of option ETF, as well as the current risks and downsides that I see. I believe that these extremely high dividend yields are powerful tools to amplify the level of distribution income generated from your portfolio when used very strategically and intentionally. Creating a large stream of income from your portfolio serves as a buffer to your financial foundation that can help you navigate changing market environments and have cash readily available to take advantage of opportunities when they arrive.

However, these sorts of option ETFs are very unconventional in how they operate. Therefore, investors shouldn't take a traditional 'buy and forget' approach with these funds. Although this approach may certainly be viable during positive market cycles, I still believe that it requires a deeper layer of investor attention. With that in mind, let's first start by reviewing the fund's strategies.

Fund Strategy & Diversity

YMAG is still a new fund that has an inception dating back to only January 2024. Despite this short history, the price and NAV consistency has held up a lot better than its single stock counterparts. This is the main appeal for me because it has been able to preserve capital over time and maintains a diverse exposure to some of the strongest tech holdings in the world. According to the most recent prospectus, YMAG sports a very reasonable management fee of only 0.29%, with acquired fund fees and expenses bringing the total annual operating expenses up to 1.28%.

Looking at the top holdings, we can see that YieldMax's NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY) has the largest weight of 16.09%. This if followed by weight in YieldMax's AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (APLY) and YieldMax's AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY), accounting for 14.82% and 14.29% respectively. However, it should be noted that the underlying positions are not actually held within these ETFs, and they instead maintain a synthetic position and make use of a synthetic option strategy. I pulled the following excerpt from page 3 of the prospectus.

As part of its synthetic covered call strategy, each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF writes (sells) call option contracts on its Underlying Security to generate income. Since the Underlying YieldMax™ ETF does not directly own Underlying Security, these written call options are sold short (i.e., selling a position it does not currently own). Each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF seeks to capture a portion of Underlying Security’s share price appreciation (generally no more than 15%) in a given month. To do so, the call options written (sold) by each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF will generally have an expiration of one month or less (the “Call Period”) and generally have a strike price that is approximately 0%-15% above the then-current Underlying Security share price.

This combination of its indirect exposure and use of a covered call option strategy effectively caps the upside price appreciation that can be experienced. The primary goal for YMAG is to capture a high rate of income by collecting the premiums from the options implemented, so there should be no expectation of price growth here. The idea is that the level of premiums that are collected are typically much during times of higher market volatility. Since the Mag 7 are growthier companies that can see large price swings, these stocks serve as a strong foundation for the synthetic option strategy. The fund's adviser, Tidal Investments, uses these elevated premium levels to fund the high rate of distributions.

The capped upside is just a side effect of using these options. To help visualize this, let's imagine that you write a call option on an asset priced at $300 with a strike price of $310. For this, you will collect a premium of $3. Now, let's imagine that the price of the underlying asset moves up to $315 per share. You would essentially collect the premium of $3 while also recognizing a price gain up to the strike price of $310 per share. That remaining $5 per share of price growth would essentially be sacrificed. Conversely, if the stock were to fall down to $295 per share, you'd see the full effect of the price loss, but this would be offset by the premium collected.

Dividend Utilization

What makes YMAG unique is that the distributions are issued out to shareholders on a weekly basis. While this is uncommon, it can serve as something that closely resembles a paycheck. This is one of the aspects that make YMAG such an attractive choice for those investors that may be looking for a way to supplement their main sources of income as a way to help fund their lifestyle expenses. However, these distributions are wildly varied and are issued at different pay rates week by week, as seen by the distribution schedule. So investors looking for a more consistent distribution should look elsewhere.

Rather than hold YMAG by itself, I believe that these distributions are best utilized when used in conjunction with a portfolio that already contains the underlying holdings of the Mag 7. These are companies with a history of innovation, rapid earnings growth, and have weak or nonexistent dividends. By using YMAG as an accompanying fund, you can effectively capture both growth and income. During bull markets, it's obviously better to hold exposure to the underlying assets that will allow you to gain from the underlying price increases. With the exception of Tesla, these companies have done exceptionally well over the last year.

Now, YMAG provides a way for us to still grow our portfolio during times where the market is flat or decreasing. Since these Mag 7 companies aren't known for their dividend yields, investors have to rely mostly on capital appreciation. Remember that saying: 'you can't have your cake and eat it too'? YMAG makes it so that you can do both. During years where the market isn't as strong and these stocks are trading sideways, YMAG is an excellent complement that allows us to now effectively profit in multiple market environments. Even during a bear market, while the price of these underlying assets may be decreasing, the income received from YMAG can offset the negative impact of any unrealized losses.

I was curious what the distribution makeup looks like for YMAG because there are some option funds that destroy their NAV due to the high rate of the distributions. However, the latest section 19a notice for September reveals that net investment income has covered 89.58% of the distributions for this fiscal year to date. Only 10.42% of the distribution has been funded by the use of return of capital.

Risk Profile

Speaking of return of capital, this form of distribution typically causes a decrease in NAV when used excessively. When the premiums collected from the options are not enough to cover the distributions, a fund may use return of capital to make up that difference. The downside is that this form of return of capital gets pulled directly from the NAV of the fund, which can contribute to a loss of value. These distributions can exacerbate a deteriorating NAV issue and add to the damage if the underlying asset is falling in share price.

A good example of this would be YieldMax's TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY). Tesla has struggled to keep up with the rest of the Mag 7 and experienced some volatile trading periods between 2023 and 2024. There was a point from the high of July 2023 where the price of TSLA fell by nearly 50% through April 2024. This poor price movement demolished TSLY's share price and ultimately made the high distribution yield of approximately 100% useless. Looking at the chart below, we can see that the price of TSLA ultimately recovered and now sits up by about 20%. However, TSLY never quite recovered because of the same underlying option strategy, which caps the upside price movements captured.

This is a real-world example of the NAV getting destroyed over time and the option strategy preventing a recovery. TSLY still issuing these high distributions alongside a falling share price only added more damage and this is a very real risk for the rest of the Mag 7 holdings. YMAG mitigates this a bit by providing a basic level of diversity. However, this diversity is essentially limited to the tech sector and there's a chance that the entire sector gets negatively impacted. This could be caused by a range of factors, like a recession, interest rate changes, higher inflation levels, or simply the results of the upcoming US election. YMAG can experience the same exact price and NAV decline in poor market conditions.

The tech sector has run up to massive premiums and may take a breather at any point. Imagine we enter a bear market in the future and the Mag 7 suffers from a year straight of declines. If all of the underlying stocks get negatively impacted, then YMAG would also experience a similar story. Since the fund is still so new and the market has remained strong, we have no idea of how this fund can navigate a down market.

This is exactly why I do not believe that YMAG is a simple buy and hold fund, no matter how bullish you are on the underlying equities. Another risk I want to address is that I see a growing trend of investors using margin out of their brokerage account to try and capitalize on the difference. For instance, I know that Fidelity has margin rates that sit around 13.075% for balances under $25k. This can vary based on your own portfolio size, but the idea here is that the huge yield of 62% would pay off the margin debt and the investor would pocket the difference. While this may sound like easy money in a green market environment, the damage could be life changing if an investor takes out too large of a margin balance and the market crashes downward.

While I am not totally against the idea, I want to put emphasis that this is a risky strategy that should be left to the investors familiar with managing this sort of risk, capital management, and have the time to actively manage their portfolio on a daily basis. The last thing you want is to be margin called during an intense bear market, and you don't have the necessary capital to get your account healthy. I plan to revisit this concept in the future and create a fully fledged out article on how to follow this sort of strategy responsibly.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that YMAG can be strategically used to boost the distributions generated from an investor's portfolio. However, I believe that a fund like YMAG should only be used if you have exposure to some of the underlying equities already. The Mag 7 stocks are growthier companies that aren't known for their amazing dividend yields. This means that investors have to rely on capital appreciation to provide the majority of the growth. However, YMAG gives us the opportunity to capture income during those weaker market periods where prices may not be growing as rapidly, enabling us to profit during different market environments.

While YMAG does eliminate some of the single stock risk that comes from other YieldMax funds, the diversity is basically limited to the tech sector. This does have a vulnerability by itself because this means that YMAG's NAV and price can deteriorate in a falling market environment. Since the fund is still new, we haven't seen how it handles this sort of market environment yet. Therefore, I rate YMAG as a buy but stay reserved with how much of a position I am taking for now.

data.title: ASGI: This 12.5% Yielder Should Be Considered By Retirement Investors
data.description:
abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (NYSE:ASGI) is a pure play infrastructure closed-end fund with a primary objective to deliver sizeable current income streams. Currently, it has over $600 million in AUM, and even though this is a relatively minor asset base from which to extract the benefit of economies of scale, the net expense ratio is acceptable at 1.65%.

It is also worth mentioning that ASGI is not that old of a fund, and has been publicly traded only since mid-2020. If we compare the total return performance of ASGI over this time period to other well-known infrastructure vehicles such as Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), we will notice quite strong correlations.

What is interesting about this chart is that, while ASGI has slightly underperformed BIP and UTF, it has exhibited much lower volatility levels.

With this context in mind, let me explain, why, in my opinion, ASGI offers an enticing investment case for income seeking investors, especially for those, who are near or already in retirement, to enhance their portfolio yields in a relatively conservative manner.

Thesis

There are four distinct aspects, which substantiate my investment thesis.

First, ASGI provides one of the highest distribution yields in the infrastructure vehicle space. Currently, the yield stands at 12.5%, which is significantly above that of BIP and UTF that offer, 4.5% and 7.1%, respectively. Just as BIP and UTF, ASGI has not cut the dividend in 2022 or 2023, when the sudden shift in the monetary policy caused many interest rate sensitive assets to massively decline. In addition, ASGI distributes the cash on a monthly basis, which in combination with truly attractive yield provides income-seeking investors a decent choice to enhance their portfolio's current income streams.

Second, the fund itself is strongly diversified not only at the individual security level, but also on the sector level. The largest exposure lies within the industrial and utility sectors, which are inherently underpinned by stable demand dynamics, especially when it comes to the underlying infrastructure assets.

Speaking of defense, we have to appreciate the beauty of infrastructure exposure, which comes with an embedded inflation-protection. In almost all cases, infrastructure investments are backed by either bankable long-term offtake agreements, which are signed before the final investment decision-making takes place or truly stable customer base, where the barriers to entry are very high (e.g., water utility service or telco companies in ASGI portfolio).

Third, as opposed to many high yielding CEFs, ASGI does not rely on external leverage to magnify its yield. This is a major advantage in terms of the risk management. It also has been one of the main reasons explaining ASGI's lower volatility levels that we saw in the chart above.

Yet, based on the annual report, ASGI has the optionality to source external leverage to a certain level:

Leverage. The Fund currently does not intend to borrow money or issue debt securities or preferred shares. The Fund is, however, permitted to borrow money or issue debt securities in an amount up to 331/3% of the value of the Fund's total assets, and issue preferred shares in an amount up to 50% of its total assets. Although it has no present intention to do so, the Fund reserves the right to borrow money from banks or other financial institutions, or issue debt securities or preferred shares, in the future if it believes that market conditions would be conducive to the successful implementation of a leveraging strategy through borrowing money or issuing debt securities or preferred shares.

In case management decides to assume debt, the chances are high that in such moment, the yield would go even higher. However, in my view, it is better to have no additional financial risk in the books given that the current yield is already attractive enough.

Fourth, since infrastructure is inherently a duration-loaded asset with a high sensitivity to interest rate changes, the fact that the future interest rate trend is clearly downward sloping (see FOMC dot plot chart below) bodes well for the income sustainability going forward.

In fact, falling interest rates should help not only from the income perspective by lowering interest costs for the underlying ASGI holdings, but also from the price appreciation angle, pushing higher infrastructure valuations.

Risks

Even though, in my view, the odds of experiencing a distribution cut down the road are very low, it is still worth factoring in two important areas of risk, which could potentially cause ASGI to revisit its distribution policy.

The first one relates to the fact that ASGI holds almost entirely pure equity exposures, which is not that characteristic to infrastructure CEFs. In many cases, we will observe an integration of some fixed income related instruments such as preferred securities or bonds, which would de-risk the exposure even further. In ASGI's situation, about 16% of the total asset base is placed in private equity securities, which tend to be even riskier than publicly traded common stocks.

While this is indeed a risk, having an equity component in the portfolio bring several benefits, such as higher income growth potential through increased dividends and the option for management to periodically monetize appreciated assets.

The second risk relates to the interest dynamics, where under a situation in which the Fed does not follow the indicated path of FOMC dot plot, or, in a worst-case scenario, decides to hike the base rates, ASGI's underlying holdings would become subject to elevated interest costs. This way, there would be a smaller margin of safety remaining in the books to weather unexpected financial shocks. The income growth potential would certainly suffer as well.

However, given the prevailing macro environment and the FOMC dot plot, experiencing a sudden change in the monetary policy or rising interest rates seems like an extremely unlikely scenario. On top of this, under such a scenario, the chances remain high that ASGI would stick to the current distribution policy since most infrastructure assets have their revenues linked to CPI escalators.

The bottom line

As outlined above, ASGI carries defensive fundamentals, which are underpinned by a diversified infrastructure asset base and no external leverage.

The benefit of an exposure towards infrastructure assets is not only bound by an attractive yield component, but it also introduces inflation-linked growth dynamics and relatively high upside from the normalization in the monetary base rates.

In my opinion, opening a minor position in abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund should be seriously considered by retirement income seeking investors, who want to enhance their portfolio yields, while accessing inflation-protected and interest rate-driven upside.

data.title: PSF: Much Improved, But No Inflation Protection
data.description:
The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:PSF) is a closed-end fund that income-focused investors can purchase as a method of achieving a very high level of current income from the assets in their portfolio. This fund is perhaps one of the more popular preferred stock funds currently in the market, which may be due to its respectable 7.26% current yield. This yield is, however, lower than many of the fund’s peers. We can see this quite clearly here:

As we can see, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund has a yield that manages to beat the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Fund. In fact, it manages to beat all four of the well-known and respected preferred stock funds from that particular manager:

Fund Name

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Opportunity Fund (PFO)

The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund also manages to outperform the domestic preferred stock index in terms of yield. Over the past twelve months, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) has paid out distributions sufficient to give it a 6.07% yield. As such, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is likely to be more attractive than at least some of the popular options for those investors who are looking to add preferred securities to their portfolios. However, the fact that its yield is quite a bit lower than three of the other funds mentioned in the first table above does reduce its appeal somewhat for those investors who are looking to achieve the maximum possible level of income from their portfolios.

Another potential problem with the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund comes from the fact that the securities in which it invests offer very limited protection against the loss of purchasing power that accompanies inflation. As I explained in a previous article, the inflation problem that has ravaged the economy for the past three years has not been solved despite policymakers’ statements to the contrary. This is one of the reasons why gold prices continue to rise:

This chart shows the price of gold over the past year. As we can clearly see, it has been rising fairly rapidly and it is up about 35.6% over the past twelve months. Alan Greenspan, the former chairman of the Federal Reserve, has made a number of remarks that suggest that he believes that the Federal Reserve should raise interest rates when the price of gold is rising. For example, in an article in Business Insider from 2016, Greenspan noted:

The abandonment of the gold standard made it possible for the welfare statists to use the banking system as a means to an unlimited expansion of credit. In the absence of the gold standard, there is no way to protect savings from confiscation through inflation. There is no safe store of value. Deficit spending is simply a scheme for the hidden confiscation of wealth. Gold stands in the way of this insidious process. It stands as a protector of property rights. If one grasps this, one has no difficulty in understanding the statists’ antagonism toward the gold standard.

Forbes also points out that Greenspan tried to stabilize the U.S. dollar against gold:

Alan Greenspan stabilized the dollar still further against gold during the 1990s, the “Greenspan gold standard.”

The way that this was accomplished was by raising interest rates when gold prices went close to $400 per ounce and reducing them when gold prices trended towards $300 per ounce. He explained this during a mid-1990s speech.

In short, the fact that gold prices have been rising is a sign that the market believes that inflation is a bigger problem than the media and policymakers want everybody to believe. The market is usually a better barometer of these things than most politicians or government bureaucrats, so the fact that gold prices have been rising so substantially over the past year is a sure sign that investors may want to take steps to protect their portfolios against inflation. Unfortunately, investing in fixed-income securities such as preferred stocks and bonds as the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund does, runs counter to this goal. As such, any investors who wish to add this fund to their portfolio should ensure that they have sufficient exposure to other assets that offer better protection against a declining U.S. dollar (the root cause of inflation).

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund in mid-May 2024. The market for fixed-income securities has generally been strong since that time, as investors purchased preferred stocks and bonds in order to lock in respectable yields for their money before the anticipated easing of monetary policy. The Federal Reserve delivered on that easing by cutting the benchmark federal funds target range by 50 basis points at its September meeting. This rate cut allowed these securities to hold onto the gains that they produced during the buying spree that took place over the summer. As the assets that are held by the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund increased over the period, we might expect that the fund’s share price will have also delivered respectable gains.

This is indeed the case as shares of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund have risen by 7.31% since our last discussion:

As we can immediately see, the fund’s performance was better than that of either the domestic preferred stock or the domestic investment-grade bond (AGG) indices. The fund did, unfortunately, underperform the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period, but this is to be somewhat expected. After all, we do not ordinarily expect fixed-income securities to outperform theoretically riskier common equities. A common rule of finance is that securities with higher risk should offer higher potential returns. Overall, the share price performance is likely to be attractive enough for most income-focused investors, even though it has declined a bit since the start of October.

However, it is important to note that investors in the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund actually do a lot better than the share price performance alone would suggest. From the previous article:

The fund’s investors have received far more gains than are visible simply by looking at the fund’s share price. This is because closed-end funds such as the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund typically pay out all of their investment profits to their owners, which results in their realized returns being much higher than the share price performance would imply.

The fund’s distributions provide a very real return for investors that we should include in any performance analysis of this fund. When we do that, we get this alternative to the chart above:

This seems almost certain to please investors in the fund, as well as attract potential investors to it. As we can see, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund delivered a 10.72% total return since the date of our last discussion. This not only beat both of the domestic fixed-income indices, but it even outperformed the S&P 500 Index. Income-focused investors are not really used to outperforming the S&P 500 Index due mostly to the generally weak performance of income securities during the zero-interest-rate era that followed the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis. As everybody likes to beat the market, this is very nice to see.

Approximately five months have passed since our last discussion on the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund, so it is reasonable to assume that some things have changed. Perhaps most importantly, the fund released a new financial report that we will want to review. This report will, after all, provide a great deal of insight into how well the fund is covering its distribution. In addition to this, we have started to see the Federal Reserve reduce interest rates, which served as a tailwind over the past few months. This article will focus specifically on these changes and update our thesis as necessary.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the primary investment objective of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. The website states that the fund will attempt to achieve this objective by investing in preferred stocks and similar securities:

Regular readers likely know that I would prefer to have a more detailed description of this fund’s investment strategy than this. Fortunately, we can find a more detailed description of the fund’s strategy in its most recent annual report. This document offers the following description:

The Fund pursues its objective primarily by investing in issues of preferred and debt securities believed to be undervalued relative to credit quality and other investment characteristics. In making this determination, the investment manager evaluates the fundamental characteristics of an issuer, including an issuer’s creditworthiness, and also takes into account prevailing market factors. In analyzing credit quality, the investment manager considers not only fundamental analysis, but also an issuer’s corporate and capital structure and the placement of the preferred or debt securities within that structure. In evaluating relative value, the investment manager also takes into account call, conversion and other structural security features, in addition to such factors as the likely directions of credit ratings and relative value versus other income security classes. The Fund will not seek to achieve specific environmental, social or governance outcomes through its portfolio of investments, nor will it pursue an overall impact or sustainable investment strategy. However, the investment manager will incorporate consideration of relevant ESG factors into its investment decision-making. For example, although the investment manager does not generally exclude investments based on ESG factors alone, when considering an investment opportunity with material exposure to carbon emissions regulation, this risk may be considered as one factor in the investment manager’s holistic review process.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its Managed Assets in a portfolio of preferred and other income securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. companies, including traditional preferred securities; hybrid preferred securities that have investment and economic characteristics of both preferred stock and debt securities; floating-rate and fixed-to-floating rate preferred securities; fixed-and floating-rate corporate debt securities; convertible securities; contingent capital securities; and securities of other open-end, closed-end or exchange-traded funds that invest primarily in preferred and/or debt securities. These securities may be across a wide range of sectors and industries.

The description goes on to state that the fund will invest at least 25% of its assets in the financials sector, which we pretty much expect any preferred stock fund to do. The description also states that up to 50% of the fund’s portfolio might be invested in non-U.S. dollar-denominated securities, which I can appreciate. As I explained in a previous article, my expectation that the U.S. dollar will decline against both gold and certain foreign currencies going forward has prompted me to want foreign currency exposure in any fixed-income fund. After all, if this view is correct, then the assets held by such a fund may experience capital gains simply due to the currency in which they are denominated rising against the U.S. dollar. This would actually result in a rising income over time as these coupons are converted back to U.S. dollars.

Unfortunately, the fund’s website does not provide a breakdown of the currencies in which the fund’s assets are denominated. However, it does provide this country allocation:

We can see that the fund’s assets consist of securities from the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, the Eurozone, Switzerland, Australia, Japan, Sweden, and Bermuda. With the possible exception of the Bermuda-issued securities, it is a fair bet that many of the non-U.S. securities are issued in a foreign currency. After all, most of these countries are respected enough in the foreign exchange market and their currencies are trusted. There is no real reason, for example, for a Swiss bank to issue securities denominated in U.S. dollars when it will have no trouble selling ones denominated in Swiss francs. The same can be said of issuers in the Eurozone, the United Kingdom, Japan, Canada, and the other locations shown on the list.

The schedule of investments shown in the fund’s semi-annual report appears to confirm this. There are securities shown in the schedule that are specifically stated to be denominated in euros and British pounds. There are no other currencies specifically mentioned in the schedule of investments, so that could mean that the fund did not have anything except for U.S. dollar-denominated securities, British pound-denominated securities, and euro-denominated securities on June 30, 2024. That does not mean that these are the only three currencies that this fund will ever have exposure to, however.

In fact, the fund’s own documentation suggests that it will have Canadian dollar exposure at certain times. We can make this assumption from the fact that it was holding some Canadian dollar forward exchange contracts on June 30, 2024:

There would be absolutely no reason for the fund to enter into currency exchange contracts unless it owned securities that would be paying it in that currency (or is planning to buy securities denominated in those currencies). We can, therefore, assume that the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund may or may not be receiving income in Canadian dollars, euros, British pounds, and U.S. dollars at any given time. There may also be some other developed market currencies that show up from time to time. If the U.S. dollar declines against these currencies going forward, that could boost the income of this fund when measured in U.S. dollar terms. Ultimately, that is something that investors would probably like to see.

The fund’s semi-annual report provides the following asset allocation as of June 30, 2024:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Preferred Securities-Exchange-Traded

24.9%

Preferred Securities-Over-the-Counter

122.2%

Money Market Funds

1.7%

As I pointed out in the previous article:

The fund’s assets consist solely of preferred stock along with a small amount of cash. We would normally expect that the fund would have a small amount of cash stored in a money market fund that it can use to pay the distribution or make investments. It is rather curious, though, that the fund has no money allocated to any other income-producing assets, such as bonds.

As I discussed in an article on the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund, some funds that advertise themselves as preferred stock funds have a substantial allocation to bonds. While bonds are usually considered to be safer than preferred stock, the inclusion of such securities is not always in the best interest of the fund’s shareholders. One reason for this is taxes, as preferred stock dividends can be eligible for income taxation at a lower rate than bond coupon payments. Thus, investors in preferred stock actually end up with more spendable income than investors in bonds, assuming identical coupon yields. As our lifestyles depend more on after-tax income than on pre-tax income, this results in preferred stock investors being able to live a more expensive lifestyle, all else being equal. It is nice to see that the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund did not follow the same path as its peers. This fund consists entirely of preferred stocks.

One thing that we do notice, though, is that the fund’s allocation to exchange-traded preferred stock declined from December 31, 2023. That was the date of the asset allocation that I provided in the previous article, as it came from the fund’s annual report. As we now have the semi-annual report available, we can see the changes that the fund made during the first half of 2024. Here is the summary:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets on December 31, 2023

% of Net Assets on June 30, 2024

% Change

Preferred Securities – Exchange-Traded

27.4%

24.9%

-2.5%

Preferred Securities – Over-the-Counter

122.2%

122.2%

No Change

Money Market Fund

0.7%

1.7%

+1.0%

We can immediately see that the changes to the fund’s portfolio during the first six months of 2024 were quite negligible. The only real change is a slight decrease in the fund’s exchange-traded preferred stock allocation and a corresponding small increase in its money market fund assets. The fund only had a 31% turnover (non-annualized) in the first six months of 2024 though, so we should not really expect major changes.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield of its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund is borrowing money and using that money to purchase preferred stock and bonds. As long as the purchased securities have a higher yield than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, this will usually be the case. It is important to keep in mind though that this strategy is much less effective today with rates at 6% than it was a few years ago when rates were near 0%. This is because the difference between the interest rate paid by the fund and the yield of the assets that it purchases is much less than it once was.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not employing too much leverage because that would expose us to an excessive amount of risk. I generally do not like to see a fund’s leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 33.78% of its overall portfolio. This is pretty much in line with the 33.60% leverage that the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which is quite surprising. After all, as we saw in the introduction, the fund’s share price has risen by 7.31% since the date that the previous article was published. That would ordinarily suggest that the fund’s portfolio increased in size, and if that is the case then its outstanding borrowings should now represent a smaller proportion of the overall portfolio than they did previously.

As has been the case with many fixed-income funds in recent months, the fund’s portfolio has not performed as well as its share price. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value from May 21, 2024 (the date that my previous article on this fund was published) through the present:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value is only up approximately 4.85% over the period. This clearly tells us that the share price has outperformed the underlying portfolio and that the fund is now more expensive than it was previously. We will discuss this later. For now, we can still see that the portfolio is larger than it used to be so the fund’s leverage should have declined more than it did. This fund might have increased its borrowing slightly to boost its gains when the underlying preferred stocks appreciated during the period.

The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund still has a leverage ratio that is pretty close to the one-third of assets level that we ordinarily deem to be acceptable. It also compares fairly well to its peers, as we can see here:

Fund Name

Leverage Ratio

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund

33.78%

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

37.63%

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Fund

37.30%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

36.91%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

37.47%

(all figures from CEF Data)

As we can see, the leverage ratio of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is below that of its peers. This is a sign that the fund is not using too much leverage as a part of its investment strategy. Overall, we should not need to worry too much about the fund’s leverage today.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. To that end, the fund pays a regular monthly distribution of $0.1260 per share ($1.5120 per share annually). This gives the fund a 7.26% yield at the current share price.

Unfortunately, this fund has not been especially consistent with respect to its distributions over the years. We can see that here:

As I stated in my previous article:

Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, this fund was a fairly reliable source of income, but the recent cuts have undoubtedly reduced its appeal in the minds of those investors who are seeking a safe and consistent income from the assets in their portfolios. The generation of income is perhaps more important today than it was in past years due to the negative impact that today’s high levels of inflation have had on our standard of living. It costs much more to maintain a certain lifestyle today than it did a few years ago, so we need our incomes to be climbing with the passage of time. This fund’s distribution cuts have been doing the exact opposite.

The fund’s current distribution is the same as it was the last time that we discussed it, so there is no need to provide an update there. However, as we saw in the previous article, the fund failed to cover its distribution over the full-year 2023 period. Let us have a look at the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, to see if things have improved at all for it.

For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund received $9,610,538 in interest and $2,285,076 in net dividends (dividends minus foreign withholding taxes). This gives the fund a total investment income of $11,895,614 for the six-month period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $6,373,904 available for shareholders. That was not sufficient to cover the $9,093,309 that the fund paid out in distributions during the period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund reported net realized gains of $2,996,327 along with $7,904,180 in net unrealized gains. The fund’s net assets increased by $8,181,102 after accounting for all inflows and outflows during the period.

As we can see, the fund managed to fully cover its distributions solely through the use of its net investment income and net realized gains during the period. This is a good improvement from the previous year’s failure to cover, but of course, 2024 is not over yet. I do not see any reason to suspect that the fund will fail to cover its distributions over the remainder of this year as its net investment income will likely come in around the same level and it has a substantial amount of unrealized gains that it could realize to cover the rest. The only real problem would be if preferred stock prices were to decline significantly, but that seems unlikely in the near term.

Overall, investors in this fund probably can rest easy as it seems unlikely that it will need to cut its payout.

Valuation

Shares of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund are currently trading at a 6.02% discount on net asset value. This is a much more attractive price than the 2.85% discount that the shares have had on average over the past month.

Expenses

The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund has an expense ratio of 4.44%, but most of this is due to the fund’s use of leverage. If we exclude its leverage costs, then it has an expense ratio of 1.28% from just the management and other fees.

Here is how this fund’s expense ratio compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Expense Ratio ex-Leverage Carrying Costs

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund

4.44%

1.28%

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

4.63%

1.47%

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Fund

5.12%

1.02%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

5.17%

1.31%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

5.19%

1.33%

(all figures from CEF Data)

We can see that when we compare the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund to other actively managed leveraged peers, its expenses are not at all bad. The fund is overall the cheapest fund on this list, and it is the second cheapest excluding leverage carrying costs.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund has delivered a fairly impressive performance to its shareholders over the past five months. Indeed, this fund’s total return actually beat the S&P 500 Index over the period. It has also corrected some of the problems that it had the last time that we discussed it, such as coverage of the distribution. However, the fund does have the same problem as other fixed-income securities in that it does not have any inherent protection against inflation as an equity fund does.

data.title: Everyone Is Trying To Call The Top
data.description:
Treasury yields rose across the curve yesterday, with the 2-year climbing back above 4%, while the 10-year rose 11 basis points to 4.18%. We have seen a significant move higher in long-term rates since the Fed cut short-term rates by 50 basis points in September. At that time, the 10-year Treasury yield was just 3.64%, but investors were overly concerned about a slowing rate of economic growth, if not a recession. Yesterday’s sharp rise was blamed on concerns over deficit spending next year, but I think the increase has more to do with normalizing the yield curve, which should continue steepening as the economy lands softly. Regardless, the knee-jerk reaction to higher bond yields is lower stock prices, and that is what we saw yesterday. I suspect there was also a healthy dose of profit taking after a six-week winning streak for the S&P 500.

That winning streak has many market pundits trying to call a top in the market, but it is a lot harder to call a top than it is to be bullish, simply because the market goes up most of the time. Regardless, pundits do it all the time in hopes of being right, despite how many times they have been wrong previously, because of the recognition it can bring if the call is accurate. Unfortunately, that has not helped investors who acted prematurely on the premonitions from these soothsayers. I don’t see a top any time soon. Instead, I see new highs coming before year-end on better-than-expected earnings growth for the third quarter of this year, followed by more significant sequential growth in the fourth quarter from the S&P 500 ex-Magnificent 7.

Earnings and stock prices have a close correlation over time, and this market is no different. Overall profits are likely to increase 7% or more on a year-over-basis for the third quarter, and that percentage should increase over the coming two quarters based on easier comparisons. That rate of change is important from the standpoint of the uptrend in equities and market momentum. No one is arguing that the stock market is cheap. Everyone knows that it is valued at the upper end of its historical range based on metrics like price-to-earnings, price-to-sales, price-to-book, as well as the widely followed CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price/Earnings multiple), which was refined by Yale University Nobel laureate Robert Shiller some 25 years ago.

Yet, valuation is a HORRIBLE timing tool for deciding when one should be in or out of the stock market. Furthermore, if investors relied solely on valuations, and particularly the CAPE, they would have greatly reduced their exposure to the stock market when it peaked in late 2021. Goldman’s market strategists, led by David Kostin, are forecasting a lost decade for the stock market that produces an annualized rate of return of just 3%, but he also sees a double-digit return in the 12 months ahead. I think he may be correct, but that just means that investors need to be tactical rather than strategic, as most passive investors are today. I will have a lot more to say about this outlook in the days ahead.

As for the coming year, I think it is important to remember that bull markets do not die of old age or from elevated valuations. They end because we have a recession, a bubble that bursts with economy-wide ramifications, or a tremendous economic shock like the doubling of oil prices or a pandemic. I don’t see a recession or a bubble on the horizon. We can always have a shock, but that is something that no one can predict. The impact of such a shock has a lot to do with the health of the economy when it occurs. On that front, we look to be in very good shape with record low unemployment, real wage growth, declining borrowing costs, and record high financial market and housing wealth.

data.title: Madison Conservative Allocation Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Recap – Third Quarter 2024

After a rough start, U.S. stocks bounced back to generate another impressive gain, with the S&P 500 Index up 5.9% for the third quarter of 2024, pushing the year-to-date total return to a staggering 22.1%, not far off 2023’s outsized 26.3% return, with three months left to go in the year.

If you hadn’t heard the term “yen carry trade” before, it was likely burned into your lexicon by early August. The rapid ascent of the formerly shunned Japanese currency was behind a nasty bout of volatility that took U.S. stocks down nearly 10%, as investors were forced to quickly unwind levered investments and prevent further losses as the yen moved against them. Behind the yen’s rise was a stark contrast in the outlook for monetary policy between the U.S. and Japan, which came to a head in the final week of July when Federal Reserve Chair Powell leaned dovish while the Bank of Japan hawkishly raised interest rates, then a sour U.S. employment report added fuel to the rate cut fire. However, the Bank of Japan yielded via softer comments around future policy and the markets went back to rallying on the belief of much easier monetary policy (lower interest rates) moving forward in the U.S.

The shifting market sentiment towards the anticipation of even lower rates propelled cyclically-based and smaller cap stocks to big gains, especially early in the quarter.

Technology stocks were notable underperformers and the mixed performance of the so-called Magnificent 7, along with other mega cap stocks led to a broader and seemingly healthier market advance. Overall, the small cap Russell 2000 Index jumped 9.3% versus the S&P 500’s 5.9% return, and the more economically sensitive Russell 1000 Value Index gained 9.4%, well above the 3.2% return for the technology-heavy Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Big gains were not just for stocks. Bond returns were boosted by a steep drop in interest rates across the yield curve from the increased expectations of deeper Fed rate cuts. Entering the quarter, investors expected the Fed Funds rate to fall just short of 0.5% by year end, and largely expected the Fed to cut by the standard 0.25% for their highly anticipated first rate cut of the cycle on September 18th. However, belief in something bigger built after Chair Powell’s comment that “the time has come for policy to adjust” during his remarks at Jackson Hole in August, as he detailed his growing confidence that inflation is under control and that the Fed is now more concerned about the cooling labor market. As the September Fed meeting approached, market odds firmed around a larger 0.5% cut, and on that fateful day, the Fed delivered with a jumbo half-point cut to kick off the cutting cycle and guided for another 0.5% of cuts to come by January. However, market optimism ran higher, exiting the quarter with an implied rate 0.75% below today’s level, with dreams of a 3% Fed Funds rate by the end of 2025. The falling interest rate hoopla drove the broad Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index to a near S&P 500 matching 5.2% return for the quarter, bringing the nine-month total up to 4.5%.

Outside of the U.S., China pushed the Bank of Japan out of the spotlight by launching a massive stimulus plan during the final week of the quarter. Investors welcomed Beijing’s willingness to turn the monetary spigot wide open by bidding Chinese equity indexes up over 20% in seven days. The upward explosion of Chinese stocks helped boost international stocks and the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index to an 8.1% return, bettering the U.S. market for the quarter.

Performance

The broadening of the market beyond the small set of very large U.S. companies benefited portfolios for the quarter. The returns of value and smaller stocks helped several of our U.S. equity positions generate outperformance to balance out the underperformance of our energy and technology allocations. Results from our international equity positioning were aided by our positions in emerging Asia but restrained by our overweight stance on Japanese and higher quality developed markets stocks. Our fixed income allocations performed roughly inline with the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index, aided by strong returns from our holdings in mortgage-backed and longer duration positions.

Our cautious stance on equity exposure, which we detail in the next section, was the largest detractor to performance.

Outlook & Positioning

Our primary rationale for remaining cautious in our asset allocation positioning hinges on the dramatic difference in market expectations and pricing between the bond and commodities markets compared to the stock market.

Starting with the bond market, as of the end of the third quarter, Fed Funds futures are pricing in nearly 2% of additional rate cuts over the next 12 months, which would bring the Fed Funds rate to around 3%. Cutting rates at that speed and magnitude has historically only occurred in a recessionary environment. The price of oil declined by 20% during the quarter, surveys of manufacturing activity remain contractionary, the index of leading economic indicators has not recorded a positive reading since February 2022, a month before the Fed began to tighten monetary policy. These are not typical signposts of a healthy economy. On the other hand, the S&P 500 charges on to repeated all-time highs, with an atypically-high valuation level and an expectation for nearly 15% earnings growth for 2025. Whatever a “soft landing” might be, we don’t believe this is it. If the bond market is correct, we are headed for an economic reckoning in the near future and stocks are due for a rude awakening. If the stock market is correct, the economic expansion is set to continue without needing a landing. In that case, it’s highly unlikely that we’ll see 2% of rate cuts over the next year and longer-term rates have fallen too far.

What’s clear is that one market has it wrong. The resolution of this condition will likely be quite volatile. We believe the bond market appears closer to fair value than stocks. As such, we remain moderately underweight equities given the highly uncertain and contradictory environment.

We truly appreciate your confidence and partnership.

Disclosures

“Madison” and/or “Madison Investments” is the unifying tradename of Madison Investment Holdings, Inc., Madison Asset Management, LLC (“MAM”), and Madison Investment Advisors, LLC (“MIA”). MAM and MIA are registered as investment advisers with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC. MFD Distributor, LLC is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a broker-dealer and is a member firm of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority. The home office for each firm listed above is 550 Science Drive, Madison, WI 53711. Madison’s toll-free number is 800-767-0300.

Upon request, Madison may furnish to the client or institution a list of all security recommendations made within the past year. Any performance data shown represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Non-deposit investment products are not federally insured, involve investment risk, may lose value and are not obligations of, or guaranteed by, any financial institution. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate.

This report is for informational purposes only and is not intended as an offer or solicitation with respect to the purchase or sale of any security.

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

The S&P 500® is an unmanaged index of large companies and is widely regarded as a standard for measuring large-cap and mid-cap U.S. stock-market performance. Results assume the reinvestment of all capital gain and dividend distributions. An investment cannot be made directly into an index.

Russell Investment Group is the source and owner of the trademarks, service marks and copyrights related to the Russell Indexes. Russell® is a trademark of Russell Investment Group.

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 11% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000® Index.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index is designed to track those securities within the broader Russell 1000 Index that FTSE Russell has determined exhibit growth characteristics.

The Russell 1000® Value Index is designed to track those securities within the broader Russell 1000 Index that FTSE Russell has determined exhibit value characteristics.

The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index captures large and mid cap representation across 22 of 23 Developed Markets countries (excluding the US) and 23 Emerging Markets countries. With 1,843 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global equity opportunity set outside the US.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index of U.S. fixed income securities. The U.S. Aggregate Index covers the USD-denominated, investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable bond market of SEC-registered securities. The index includes bonds from the Treasury, Government-Related, Corporate, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM passthroughs), ABS, and CMBS sectors.

The Magnificent Seven stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla.

Yield Curve is a line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. There are three main types of yield curve shapes: normal (upward sloping curve), inverted (downward sloping curve) and flat.

A basis point is one hundredth of a percent.

Duration is a measure of interest rate sensitivity. For example, a portfolio with a duration of three years would be expected to rise 3% if interest rates were to fall 1%. It is measured in years because it can also be used to measure the time it takes to recover one’s original investment taking all cash flows into account.

Please consult with your financial advisor to determine your risk tolerance and investment objectives. While Madison constructs portfolios for various risk tolerances, its Asset Allocation Team does not determine individual client’s risk tolerance or investment objectives.

All investing involves risks including the possible loss of principal. There can be no assurance the asset allocation portfolios will achieve their investment objectives. The portfolios may invest in equities which are subject to market volatility. In addition to the general risk of investing, the portfolio is subject to additional risks including investing in bond and debt securities, which includes credit risk, prepayment risk and interest rate risk. When interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. Securities rated below investment grade are more sensitive

to economic, political and adverse development changes. International equities involve risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and differences in accounting standards.

Holdings may vary depending on account inception date, objective, cash flows, market volatility, and other variables. Any securities identified and described herein do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold, and these securities may not be purchased for a new account. There is no guarantee that any securities transactions identified and described herein were, or will be profitable.

Although the information in this report has been obtained from sources that the firm believes to be reliable, we do not guarantee its accuracy, and any such information may be incomplete or condensed. All opinions included in the report constitute the authors’ judgment as of the date of this report and are subject to change without notice.
